coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of indium-tin-oxide free, all-solution-processed flexible nanogenerator device using nanocomposite of barium titanate and graphene quantum dots in polyvinylidene fluoride polymer matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Bakar, Elyani Abu
Mohamed, Mohd Ambri
Ooi, Poh Choon
Wee, M.F. Mohd Razip
Dee, Chang Fu
Majlis, Burhanuddin Yeop
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrated the feasibility of indium-tin-oxide free, solution processable flexible nanogenerator using the nanocomposite mixture consists of polyvinylidene fluoride polymer, barium titanate and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the 2.0 cm by 2.5 cm polyethylene terephthalate substrate. Poly (3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly (styrenesulfonate) was used as the bottom conductive layer to replace the commonly used ITO bottom electrode. The multi-stacking layers were deposited using spin-coating method and the top metal electrode contacts were formed by spray-coating technique. Nanogenerator device integrated with GQDs was fabricated to examine the influence of the GQDs towards the electrical performance by compared to reference device without the presence of GQDs. Significant higher output voltage was obtained from transverse mode deformation for nanogenerator device with 4.6 Vpeak-to-peak and 48 m s wider width of positive polarity output signal. It might be associated to the presence of GQDs in enhancing the higher output voltage by charges trapped effect at the interface between GQD fillers and PVDF phases. The fabricated nanogenerator device was able to light up a light emitting diode when connected to external circuit and being stable up to 60 h without significant degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanogenerator
Polyvinylidene fluoride
Graphene quantum dots
Barium titanate
Mechanical deformation
β-phase

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in-part by research grant LRGS/2015/UKM-UKM/NANOMITE/04/01 from the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia and the Top Down Research Grant ( TD-2015-002 ). E.A.B appreciates the Federal Training Scholarship Scheme for Postgraduate Studies from the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the fabricated nanogenerator device. The inset shows the SEM image cross-sectional structure of the Nanogenerator-Device. (b) Surface morphology of the nanocomposite layer.

(A) XRD patterns of Nanogenerator-Device (pink line) and Reference Device (black line) samples indicating the prominent peak of β-phase of PVDF, tetragonal structure of BaTiO3 and existence of GQDs. (b) Raman spectra of Nanogenerator-Device indicating the existence of GQDs in the active layer.

Open circuit output voltage measurements by longitudinal (d31) mode generator of a) Nanogenerator-Device and b) Reference-Device followed by the magnified views of the selected regions in (a) and (b) are shown below. The inset in magnified view of (a) illustrates (d31) mode generator.

Open circuit output voltage measurements by transverse (d33) mode generator of a) Nanogenerator-Device and b) Reference-Device followed by the magnified views of the selected regions in (a) and (b) are shown below. The inset in magnified view of (a) illustrates (d33) mode generator.
External circuit that constructed to (a) measure the efficiency of the system and (b) LED light up application.
Stability test measurement on Nanogenerator-Device with selected region magnified view on the right.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of indium-tin-oxide free, all-solution-processed flexible nanogenerator device using nanocomposite of barium titanate and graphene quantum dots in polyvinylidene fluoride polymer matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics (IMEN), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrated the feasibility of indium-tin-oxide free, solution processable flexible nanogenerator using the nanocomposite mixture consists of polyvinylidene fluoride polymer, barium titanate and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the 2.0 cm by 2.5 cm polyethylene terephthalate substrate. Poly (3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly (styrenesulfonate) was used as the bottom conductive layer to replace the commonly used ITO bottom electrode. The multi-stacking layers were deposited using spin-coating method and the top metal electrode contacts were formed by spray-coating technique. Nanogenerator device integrated with GQDs was fabricated to examine the influence of the GQDs towards the electrical performance by compared to reference device without the presence of GQDs. Significant higher output voltage was obtained from transverse mode deformation for nanogenerator device with 4.6 Vpeak-to-peak and 48 m s wider width of positive polarity output signal. It might be associated to the presence of GQDs in enhancing the higher output voltage by charges trapped effect at the interface between GQD fillers and PVDF phases. The fabricated nanogenerator device was able to light up a light emitting diode when connected to external circuit and being stable up to 60 h without significant degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of piezoelectric energy harvesting study has gained explosive attention recently because of the unique property of piezoelectric effect that allows materials to convert mechanical energy to electrical energy as a response to the external stimulus [1,2]. Only certain polymers and ceramics fillers could demonstrate the piezoelectric behavior. Poly-vinylidene fluoride (PVDF), the most investigated electroactive polymers for possible sensors, actuators and energy harvesting applications as compared to few polymers that also presenting piezoelectricity such as Nylon-11 and polyactic acid owing to it has the best all-around electroactive properties of β-phase, the highest electrical dipole moment of the PVDF monomer unit attributed to the electronegativity of fluorine and as compared to the hydrogen and carbon atoms [3,4]. The ordered state in β-phase is highly essential to the demonstration of large polarization and high piezoelectric sensitivity [5].

The inclusion of piezoelectric ceramic fillers, such as barium titanate (BaTiO3) [3], lead zirconate titanate (PZT) [6], Pb(Sc1/2Nb1/2)O3]-Pb(Mg1/3Nb2/3)O3-PbTiO3 [7], (1–x)Pb(Mg1/3Nb2/3)O3–xPbTiO3 [8] and lead titanate (PbTiO3) [9] within the polymer matrix, for example PVDF to form the electroactive composites is being proposed to overcome the undesired structural deformations or microstructural limitations induced by methods used to obtain β-PVDF that might hinder the mentioned applications [4]. The inclusion of nanosize ceramic particles can increase the dielectric constant with increasing filler content and additionally stimulate the nucleation of the electroactive phase of the polymer and promotes the possibility of tailoring the piezoelectric response by proper tuning the response of polymer and filler to meet the requirements of intended use [10,11]. Among the reported ceramic materials, BaTiO3 is being widely studied due to it is lead free and less harmful to the environment [12].

Meanwhile, the approach of dispersed graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the filler to increase the dielectric constant of electroactive polymer materials also being carried out recently as an alternate option besides the inclusion of piezoelectric ceramic materials into the electroactive polymer matrix [13]. The introducing of this advanced multifunctional material GQDs into the development of nanogenerator devices because of its unique optical, electronic, spintronic, and photoelectric properties induced by the quantum confinement effect and edge effect [14]. In addition, its fragments limited in size, or domains, of a single-layer two-dimensional layered structure with a large aspect ratio thus easier to form a large number of parallel micro-capacitors within the polymer matrix [15,16].

Therefore, the two different fillers, i.e. BaTiO3 and GQD will be introduced into the PVDF matrix to form the piezoelectric nanocomposite in this study as it is rarely reported to the best of our knowledge. The nanocomposite layer with different GQDs concentration and fixed mixing ratio of PVDF and BaTiO3 will be used to fabricate the all-solution-processed nanogenerator device on flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate. The nanogenerator device is deliberately fabricated without indium-tin-oxide (ITO) by using simple solution processed route to deposit the bottom and top conductive electrodes. Poly (3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) will be used as bottom conductive electrode to replace the commonly use ITO electrode as the PEDOT:PSS film is believed to be a better durable electrode that could operate for 10 h at 1 MHz without electrode damage as compared to ITO electrode that can damage easily when operating at a frequency of 213 Hz [17].

In this study, GQDs in ethanol (1 mg/ml) was purchased from ACS Material (CAS No.7440-40-0; Product No. GNQD0101). BaTiO3 solution was prepared by dissolving 0.6 g Sigma Aldrich-Barium Titanate (IV) nanopowder (cubic crystalline phase, CAS No. 12047-27-7; Product No. 338842) <100 nm particle size (BET), ≥99% trace metal basis in 2 ml ethanol followed by 3 h of ultra-sonication at room temperature. Whereas, PVDF solution with high order β-phase content of PVDF while sustaining the tetragonal structure for BaTiO3 [3] was prepared by dissolving 0.25 mg Poly (Vinylidene Fluoride) average Mw ∼534,000 b y GPC powder from Sigma-Aldrich (CAS No. 24937-79-9; Product No. 182702) in 2 ml ethanol and stirred at 350 rpm for 15 min at room temperature.

Fabrication of the flexible piezoelectric nanogenerator device began with 1 min oxygen plasma treatment at 15 W on the 2.0 cm × 2.5 cm polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate to reduce the hydrophobicity of the substrate's surface prior to the deposition of 80 nm PEDOT:PSS (AL4083 used as purchased from Ossila) at 2000 rpm for 60 s. 120 μl methanol drop method treatment had been performed immediately after the spin coating to enhance the conductivity performance of the bottom conductive layer before 70 °C annealing in 20 min to eliminate the hydrophilic insulator PSS from the coated layer [17].

Next, the 100 nm nanocomposite layer was deposited on top of the PEDOT: PSS layer at 2000 rpm for 60 s. The nanocomposite mixture was prepared by mixing the PVDF, BaTiO3 and GQDs solutions in the ratio of 1:2:1.5 and labelled as Nanogenerator-Device to study the influence of GQDs towards the electrical performance by comparing to the reference device without GQDs and labelled as Reference-Device. Finally, the top electrode metal contact was formed by spray coating the silver nanowires (AgNWs) in isopropyl alcohol with the aid of 0.75 cm × 0.5 cm shadow mask on an 85 °C hot plate.

The structural properties of the devices were characterized by field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffractometer (XRD) 2 Powder 45 and Confocal Micro-Raman Spectroscopy. The electrical performances of output voltage-time generated from the devices were measured by digital oscilloscope (DSO 9404 A Agilent). The generator configuration is driven from the forces of two fingers tenses in longitudinal mode (d31) and two fingers knocking, in transverse mode (d33). For all electrical measurements, PEDOT: PSS was connected to negative terminal and AgNWs electrode was connected to positive terminal according to the polarity switching examination [18] and the amplitude of input signal is the maximum voltage caused by the deformation relative to the equilibrium state when the force is removed. The input signal deviation of the devices for both deformations, i.e. d31 and d33 were computed as 0.1356 and 0.1711, respectively. Whereas the frequency can be estimated from the time taken for the voltage to rise and fall relative to the equilibrium state during the press-release deformation.

Fig. 1 (a) illustrates the schematic diagram of the fabricated device in the structure of AgNWs/Nanocomposite/PEDOT:PSS/PET with the inset shows the SEM crossectional structure of the Nanogenerator-Device with the approximate 200 nm formed by PEDOT-PSS and nanocomposite layer. Uniformly distributed fine grain formed by nanocomposite can be seen clearly in Fig. 1(b). Fig. 2 (a) shows the XRD characterization of the fabricated devices to ensure the GQDs, β-phase, and the tetragonal structure presence in the device. Fig. 2(a) shows the presence of the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20.11° indicating the β-phase of PVDF as reported by others [19]. For BaTiO3, the tetragonal structure were existence at (001) crystal planes on the diffraction spectrum of 22.07° respectively [20]. Moreover, the presence of GQDs alone exhibits a broad diffraction peaks from 16.56° to 23.21° as reported in literature [21] due to the overlapping by other particles. The existence of GQD in the device was further confirmed by Raman Spectroscopy measurement as shown in Fig. 2(b). The characteristic peaks for GQD in nanocomposite layer of Nanogenerator-Device was detected at 1357 and 1590 cm−1 indicating D-band and G-band that refer to the disordered sp2 carbon and well-ordered graphite, respectively [22].

Inset in Fig. 3 shows the generated output voltage by two fingers tenses in longitudinal mode (d31) with a constant acceleration 0.14 ms−2 at 17.8° repetition bending and a 13.1 mN applied force on a moveable beam. Mean peak-to-peak-voltage (Vpp) of d31 deformation measured during open circuit for Nanogenerator-Device and Reference-Device could be achieved up to 1.6 V and 0.7 V, respectively as shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b). The larger voltage fluctuations are being observed for Nanogenerator-Device as shown in Fig. 3(a) as compared to Reference Device in Fig. 3 (b). This observation might be attributed to the role of GQDs in enhancing the output voltage performance. It is reflected with significant signal phase response generated due to the lattice vibration during external mechanical stimuli and could be seen clearly from repeatable signal of phase response as shown in inset of Fig. 3 (a) for Nanogenerator-Device but almost not observable in inset of Fig. 3 (b) for the Reference-Device.

Fig. 4 shows the output voltage generated from Nanogenerator-Device and Reference-Device respectively by applying d33 deformation, the two fingers knocking because the generated low output voltage by applying d31 deformation was impractical for simple real applications such as lighting up the light emitting diode (LED). The applied force and acceleration speed for d33 deformation were set at 265 mN and 2.73 m s−2, respectively as shown in the depicted picture in inset of Fig. 4 (a). Fig. 4(a) and (b) show the output voltage generated from Nanogenerator-Device and Reference-Device achieved up to 4.6 and 4.26 Vpp, respectively. The higher output waveform was obtained for d33 deformation as compared to d31 deformation suggested that higher forces and periodic cycles caused by piezotronic effect of active element materials in the nanocomposite mixture [23,24]. Besides that, different thicker repetition intensities of output waveform can be seen clearly in Fig. 4(a) and (b) where Nanogenerator-Device generated higher waveform repetition as compared to Reference-Device while the changes from unexcited state, i.e. the initial state without deformation to the excitation state as marked in the inset of Fig. 4 (a); having 48 m s wider width of positive polarity output signal generated by Nanogenerator-Device was obtained as compared to 6.5 m s width for Reference-Device as shown in the inset of Fig. 4 (b). The wider width of the waveform in Fig. 4(a) could be associated to charges trapped at the interface between GQD fillers and PVDF phases [13]. The probable explanation could be the increase of high density transient flow of free carriers due to interfacial polarization will increase the density of free carriers to a certain value of Fermi level moves up above the various electron trapping levels occurred and the carriers might transport from the polymer layer are trapped at the various energy levels in the GQD charge trapping site and yielded higher output voltage was achieved [25] during the external deformation. Then, the trapped chargers were being detrapped when the applied force was removed. The higher excitation state duration will be preferable for real applications.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the electrical circuit that constructed to demonstrate the capability of the fabricated Nanogenerator-Device to light up a red LED by d33 deformation. The output current density at that instance was calculated to be 4.13 pAcm−2 with power density of 11.2 μWcm−3 after 79 s of deformation. In order to examine the quality indicator of the entire system, a circuit [26] has been constructed as shown in Fig. 5 (b) to measure the efficiency of the system. The collected charge will pass through C1 before transferred to bridge rectifier and C2. The efficiency was obtained as 49.38% based on the ratio between the total mechanical energy to charge up C1 and C2 [27]. Subsequently, Fig. 6 (a) shows the harvested output voltage performance from 3000 vibration cycles per minute by attaching the device to a 5 V, 25 mA motorized vibrator for 60 h consecutively to investigate the stability performance of the fabricated Nanogenerator-Device using the same circuit in Fig. 5 (b). It can be seen from Fig. 6 that the device maintains the generated voltage at 3.4 Vpp with 2.46 nJ harvested energy without significant degradation during the 60 h stability aging test. Inset in Fig. 6 shows the magnified views of the voltage output waveform at the selected region.

We demonstrated the feasibility of solution processed route to fabricate ITO-free nanogenerator using nanocomposite consisting of PVDF, BaTiO3 and GQDs. PEDOT-PSS was used to replace ITO as a bottom conductive layer. The longitudinal and transverse modes of external mechanical stimuli were applied to investigate the influence of GQDs towards the electrical performance of the fabricated Nanogenerator-Device as compared to the Reference-Device. It is found that the transverse mode stimuli generated higher output voltage as compared to longitudinal stimuli due to higher applied force. Significant higher output voltage was obtained from transverse mode deformation for Nanogenerator-Device compared to Reference-Device with 4.6 and 4.26 Vpp were achieved, respectively. It is believed that the higher output voltage attained by Nanogenerator-Device might be associated to the presence of GQDs in enhancing the higher output voltage and could be further verified by the 48 m s wider width of positive polarity output signal generated by Nanogenerator-Device and 6.5 m s width by the Reference-Device. The wider width of the waveform might be contributed by the charges trapped at the interface between GQD fillers and PVDF phases. The fabricated Nanogenerator-Device was able to light up a LED when connected to external circuit and being stable up to 60 h without significant degradation. Therefore, the fabricated nanogenerator devices show promising applications in low-power self-biased electronic devices, pressure sensor, and energy conversion sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in-part by research grant LRGS/2015/UKM-UKM/NANOMITE/04/01 from the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia and the Top Down Research Grant (TD-2015-002). E.A.B appreciates the Federal Training Scholarship Scheme for Postgraduate Studies from the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia.
